food_name: Thick Crust Pizza with Meat Toppings
aliases: Thick Crust Pizza with Meat Toppings
Pizza with meat toppings thick crust

english: Thick Crust Pizza with Meat Toppings
Pizza with meat toppings thick crust

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic pizza class (FOODON_00002473). No specific subclass for thick-crust meat pizza exists; broader class used as the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP covers pizza as a category. No specific facet code for thick-crust meat variant; broader category code used as the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept for thick-crust meat pizza identified in the clinical terminology hierarchy.

main_ingredients: processed meat toppings (pepperoni, salami, or similar)

serving.description: 1 slice (approximately 1/8 of a standard 30 cm pizza)
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving is one slice of a standard 30 cm thick-crust meat pizza, weighing approximately 120 g, providing around 318 kcal.

nutrient_source.title: Thick Crust Pizza with Meat — Singapore HPB Foodo Database (CR ID F05020112)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre) sourced directly from HPB Foodo record F05020112 at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for pizza with meat toppings (USDA SR Legacy #21299) as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown for this item; adaptation documented in notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 80 is based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for pizza with refined wheat flour crust (white flour pizza base, GI ~80, reference food glucose=100). Thick-crust pizza with meat toppings is expected to have a similar or slightly higher GI than standard pizza due to the greater proportion of refined flour dough. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (80) × carbohydrate per serving (33.4 g for 120 g serving) / 100 = 26.7, rounded to 26.8.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 598 mg per 100 g — driven by processed meat toppings, cheese, and tomato sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals managing hypertension should limit or avoid thick-crust meat pizza; sodium at 598 mg/100 g (approximately 718 mg per 120 g slice) approaches or exceeds the recommended single-meal sodium ceiling of 600–800 mg, and processed meat toppings such as pepperoni and salami are primary contributors that cannot be reduced by preparation adjustments.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High GI (~80) refined flour crust with low dietary fibre (1.18 g/100 g) producing a high glycemic load of ~26.8 per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit thick-crust meat pizza; the refined flour base has a high GI of approximately 80 and the low dietary fibre content of 1.18 g/100 g provides minimal glycaemic buffering, resulting in a high glycemic load (~26.8 per 120 g slice) that is likely to cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~4.5 g/100 g), sodium (598 mg/100 g), and estimated trans fat (0.1 g/100 g) from processed meat toppings
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit thick-crust meat pizza due to the combination of elevated saturated fat (~4.5 g/100 g from cheese and processed meats), high sodium (598 mg/100 g), and estimated trans fat (0.1 g/100 g) from processed meat toppings, all of which contribute to atherogenic risk and blood pressure elevation.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pro-inflammatory processed meat toppings high in arachidonic acid and saturated fat; estimated trans fat 0.1 g/100 g; high glycaemic load from refined flour crust
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid thick-crust meat pizza; processed meat toppings (pepperoni, salami) are high in arachidonic acid and saturated fat which promote inflammatory pathways, estimated trans fat at 0.1 g/100 g is pro-inflammatory at any amount, and the high glycaemic load from the refined flour crust further drives systemic inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Thick Crust Pizza with Meat Toppings is a baked pizza made with a thick, doughy base of refined wheat flour, topped with tomato sauce, mozzarella cheese, and processed meat toppings such as pepperoni, salami, or similar cured meats. The thick crust is characteristically soft and chewy on the inside with a lightly crisped exterior, and the generous dough layer gives the pizza a bread-like quality that distinguishes it from thin-crust varieties. The flavour profile is rich, savoury, and umami-forward, driven by the combination of melted cheese, seasoned tomato sauce, and the salty, spiced character of the processed meats. Per 100 g, this pizza provides 265 kcal, 13.7 g protein, 11.0 g fat, 27.85 g carbohydrate, 1.18 g dietary fibre, and 598 mg sodium. The high sodium content (598 mg/100 g) is a primary clinical concern for hypertension management. The refined flour crust has a high glycaemic index of approximately 80 with a glycaemic load of ~26.8 per 120 g slice, making it unsuitable for blood glucose management in Type 2 Diabetes. The saturated fat from cheese and processed meats (~4.5 g/100 g) and estimated trans fat (0.1 g/100 g) from processed meats raise cardiovascular risk. Pizza is widely consumed in Singapore and globally as a casual dining, takeaway, and delivery meal, and is commonly available in fast-food chains and pizza restaurants. It is classified under grains and staples / bread-based dishes in the Singapore HPB food database.

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Foodo record F05020112 at confidence 0.95 (exact match). Saturated fat adapted from USDA SR Legacy closest equivalent (pizza with meat toppings). GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous entry for white flour pizza base. All sources are approved under Project Veritas v2 source policy.
notes: 1. Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre) are sourced directly from HPB Foodo record F05020112 (confidence 0.95, exact match). 2. Saturated fat (4.5 g/100 g) is adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for pizza with meat toppings (closest approved-source equivalent); HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown for this item. 3. Trans fat (0.1 g/100 g) is an estimate based on the presence of processed meat toppings (pepperoni, salami); this is consistent with the input record's health suitability notes and USDA data for similar products. 4. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown not available in HPB Foodo for this record, and USDA SR Legacy does not provide a sufficiently close match for these sub-fractions at the composite dish level. 5. potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for this composite dish are not reported in HPB Foodo record F05020112 and a sufficiently close composite match is not available in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for all these fields. 6. GI of 80 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for white flour pizza base; no direct GI measurement for thick-crust meat pizza was identified in the approved GI sources. 7. This is a Western cuisine food; multilingual naming requirements apply only to Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, and Vietnamese dishes per Project Veritas v2 rules. No local-language names are required or populated. 8. Occasion tag 'breakfast' from the input record is not carried forward as thick-crust meat pizza is not a conventional breakfast food; meal context updated to casual dining, takeaway, delivery, lunch, and dinner.
